tasks.prompt: [Warm-up] Install Vertical Pod Autoscaler (VPA) and query recommended resource values for a Deployment in recommender mode. Create a Deployment with intentionally low requests/limits, then create a VPA object in 'Off' mode (recommend only, no automatic changes) and inspect the recommended CPU and memory values.
tasks.starter:
# 1. Install VPA (metrics-server must be running first)
kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kubernetes/autoscaler/master/vertical-pod-autoscaler/deploy/vpa-v1-crd-gen.yaml
kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kubernetes/autoscaler/master/vertical-pod-autoscaler/deploy/vpa-rbac.yaml

# Or use the official script:
# git clone https://github.com/kubernetes/autoscaler.git
# cd autoscaler/vertical-pod-autoscaler
# ./hack/vpa-up.sh

# 2. Test Deployment with intentionally low requests
kubectl apply -f - <<'EOF'
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: vpa-demo
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: vpa-demo
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: vpa-demo
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: stress
        image: polinux/stress
        args: ["--cpu", "1", "--timeout", "600"]
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: "10m"      # intentionally low
            memory: "10Mi"
EOF

# 3. Create VPA object (Off mode = recommend only)
apiVersion: autoscaling.k8s.io/v1
kind: VerticalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: vpa-demo-vpa
spec:
  targetRef:
    apiVersion: "apps/v1"
    kind: Deployment
    name: vpa-demo
  updatePolicy:
    updateMode: "___"   # Off, Initial, Recreate, or Auto

# 4. Check recommendations after a few minutes
kubectl describe vpa vpa-demo-vpa
tasks.solution:
# 3. Create VPA in Off mode
kubectl apply -f - <<'EOF'
apiVersion: autoscaling.k8s.io/v1
kind: VerticalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: vpa-demo-vpa
spec:
  targetRef:
    apiVersion: "apps/v1"
    kind: Deployment
    name: vpa-demo
  updatePolicy:
    updateMode: "Off"
EOF

# 4. Check recommendations after a few minutes
kubectl describe vpa vpa-demo-vpa
# Sample output:
# Recommendation:
#   Container Recommendations:
#     Container Name: stress
#     Lower Bound:
#       Cpu:    100m
#       Memory: 262144k
#     Target:
#       Cpu:    587m
#       Memory: 262144k
#     Uncapped Target:
#       Cpu:    587m
#       Memory: 262144k
#     Upper Bound:
#       Cpu:    1
#       Memory: 262144k

# 5. Switch to Auto mode to observe automatic application
kubectl patch vpa vpa-demo-vpa --type='json' -p='[{"op": "replace", "path": "/spec/updatePolicy/updateMode", "value": "Auto"}]'

# After a moment Pods are recreated with the new requests
kubectl get pods -l app=vpa-demo -o custom-columns='NAME:.metadata.name,CPU-REQ:.spec.containers[0].resources.requests.cpu,MEM-REQ:.spec.containers[0].resources.requests.memory'
tasks.explanation: Unlike HPA, VPA adjusts the CPU/memory requests size of each Pod rather than the number of Pods. updateMode: Off computes recommendations without modifying any Pods. Switching to Auto mode causes VPA to evict and recreate Pods with the new requests applied. HPA is suited for scale-out (horizontal scaling); VPA is suited for scale-up (vertical scaling). Using both on the same Deployment at the same time causes conflicts.
hints: VPA recommendations take a few minutes to appear. metrics-server must be running and the Pods must be under load.
If the polinux/stress image is unavailable, substitute with: kubectl run load-gen --image=busybox -- sh -c "while true; do :; done"
Applying VPA and HPA to the same Deployment simultaneously causes conflicts: VPA raises requests, HPA sees high CPU utilization and scales out, creating a loop.

tasks.prompt: [Core Practice] Install KEDA (Kubernetes Event-Driven Autoscaling) and create a CPU-based ScaledObject to observe scale-out and scale-in as load changes. Monitor Pod count changes and ScaledObject status before and after generating load.
tasks.starter:
# 1. Install KEDA
helm repo add kedacore https://kedacore.github.io/charts
helm repo update
helm install keda kedacore/keda --namespace keda --create-namespace

# 2. Create a test Deployment
kubectl apply -f - <<'EOF'
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: keda-demo
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: keda-demo
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: keda-demo
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: app
        image: nginx
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: 100m
          limits:
            cpu: 200m
EOF

# 3. Create a ScaledObject (scale out when CPU > 50%)
apiVersion: keda.sh/v1alpha1
kind: ScaledObject
metadata:
  name: keda-demo-scaler
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    name: ___              # target Deployment name
  minReplicaCount: ___     # minimum Pod count
  maxReplicaCount: ___     # maximum Pod count
  triggers:
  - type: cpu
    metricType: Utilization
    metadata:
      value: "50"          # scale out when CPU > 50%
tasks.solution:
# 3. Create the ScaledObject
kubectl apply -f - <<'EOF'
apiVersion: keda.sh/v1alpha1
kind: ScaledObject
metadata:
  name: keda-demo-scaler
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    name: keda-demo
  minReplicaCount: 1
  maxReplicaCount: 5
  cooldownPeriod: 30
  triggers:
  - type: cpu
    metricType: Utilization
    metadata:
      value: "50"
EOF

# 4. Check ScaledObject status
kubectl get scaledobject keda-demo-scaler
# NAME                SCALETARGETKIND      SCALETARGETNAME   MIN   MAX   TRIGGERS   READY   ACTIVE
# keda-demo-scaler    apps/v1.Deployment   keda-demo         1     5     cpu        True    False

# 5. Generate load (in a separate terminal)
kubectl run -it load-gen --rm --image=busybox --restart=Never -- \
  sh -c "while true; do wget -q -O- http://keda-demo; done"

# 6. Watch Pod count change (in another terminal)
kubectl get pods -l app=keda-demo -w
# NAME                        READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
# keda-demo-xxxx              1/1     Running   0          2m
# keda-demo-yyyy              0/1     Pending   0          5s   <-- scaling out
# keda-demo-yyyy              1/1     Running   0          8s

# After stopping load, check ScaledObject status
kubectl describe scaledobject keda-demo-scaler | grep -A10 'Conditions:'
tasks.explanation: KEDA extends HPA to support scaling based on external event sources (Kafka message count, Redis queue length, Prometheus metrics, etc.) in addition to CPU and memory. A ScaledObject automatically creates and manages an HPA object. Setting minReplicaCount: 0 enables scale-to-zero, reducing Pods to none when there are no events and saving cost. cooldownPeriod is the wait time before scaling in, preventing unnecessary oscillation.
hints: Run kubectl get hpa to see the HPA object KEDA creates automatically. Deleting the ScaledObject also deletes the HPA.
The CPU trigger relies on metrics-server internally, so metrics-server must be installed.
If the ScaledObject READY column is False, check the Conditions section of kubectl describe scaledobject <name> for the reason.

tasks.prompt: [Application] Use KEDA's ScaledJob to set up event-driven scaling that creates a Job for each message in a Redis List queue. Deploy Redis, push messages to the queue, and simulate a scenario where a Job is created and processed for each message.
tasks.starter:
# 1. Deploy Redis
kubectl run redis --image=redis --port=6379
kubectl expose pod redis --port=6379 --name=redis-service

# 2. TriggerAuthentication (Redis connection info)
apiVersion: keda.sh/v1alpha1
kind: TriggerAuthentication
metadata:
  name: redis-auth
spec:
  secretTargetRef: []   # empty array if Redis has no password

# 3. Create a ScaledJob
apiVersion: keda.sh/v1alpha1
kind: ScaledJob
metadata:
  name: queue-worker
spec:
  jobTargetRef:
    template:
      spec:
        containers:
        - name: worker
          image: busybox
          command: ["sh", "-c", "echo 'Processing job'; sleep 10; echo 'Done'"]
        restartPolicy: Never
  minReplicaCount: ___
  maxReplicaCount: ___
  triggers:
  - type: redis
    metadata:
      address: redis-service:6379
      listName: job-queue
      listLength: "1"   # 1 Job per queue item

# 4. Push messages to the queue and watch Jobs being created
kubectl exec redis -- redis-cli rpush job-queue "task1" "task2" "task3"
kubectl get jobs -w
tasks.solution:
# 1. Deploy Redis
kubectl run redis --image=redis --port=6379
kubectl expose pod redis --port=6379 --name=redis-service

# 2. Create the ScaledJob
kubectl apply -f - <<'EOF'
apiVersion: keda.sh/v1alpha1
kind: ScaledJob
metadata:
  name: queue-worker
spec:
  jobTargetRef:
    template:
      spec:
        containers:
        - name: worker
          image: busybox
          command: ["sh", "-c", "echo 'Processing job'; sleep 5; echo 'Done'"]
        restartPolicy: Never
  minReplicaCount: 0
  maxReplicaCount: 5
  triggers:
  - type: redis
    metadata:
      address: redis-service.default.svc.cluster.local:6379
      listName: job-queue
      listLength: "1"
EOF

# 3. Push 3 messages to the queue
kubectl exec redis -- redis-cli rpush job-queue "task1" "task2" "task3"
# (integer) 3

# 4. Watch Jobs being created
kubectl get jobs -w
# NAME                 COMPLETIONS   DURATION   AGE
# queue-worker-xxxxx   0/1                      2s   <-- task1
# queue-worker-yyyyy   0/1                      2s   <-- task2
# queue-worker-zzzzz   0/1                      2s   <-- task3
# queue-worker-xxxxx   1/1           8s         8s   <-- completed

# 5. Confirm no more Jobs are created when queue is empty
kubectl exec redis -- redis-cli llen job-queue
# (integer) 0

# 6. Clean up
kubectl delete scaledjob queue-worker
kubectl delete scaledobject keda-demo-scaler
kubectl delete deploy keda-demo
kubectl delete pod redis
kubectl delete service redis-service
tasks.explanation: ScaledJob creates Jobs to process a work queue rather than scaling a Deployment. When messages accumulate in the Redis List, Jobs are created in proportion to listLength, and they are destroyed upon completion. This pattern is ideal for batch processing, image conversion, email sending, and other queue-based tasks. KEDA makes it easy to integrate with diverse event sources such as Kafka, RabbitMQ, Azure Service Bus, and AWS SQS.
hints: If Redis is in a different namespace, specify the address as '<service>.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local:<port>'.
ScaledJob's maxReplicaCount is the maximum number of concurrently running Jobs. Even if 100 messages are in the queue, only up to maxReplicaCount Jobs run simultaneously.
Completed Jobs are not automatically removed. Use successfulJobsHistoryLimit and failedJobsHistoryLimit to control how many are retained.
